key_messages: Dry and seasonably warm weather will continue through the end of the week as high pressure settles over the region.
Showers and thunderstorms are likely to return to the region next week as a series of shortwaves cross the Northeast, ushering in seasonably cooler conditions by midweek.
afd.what_changed: As of 223 AM EDT Thursday... No significant changes were made with this forecast.
afd.raw:
000
FXUS61 KBTV 231051
AFDBTV

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Burlington VT
651 AM EDT Thu Jul 23 2026

.WHAT HAS CHANGED...
As of 223 AM EDT Thursday...

No significant changes were made with this forecast.

&&

.KEY MESSAGES...
As of 223 AM EDT Thursday...

1. Dry and seasonably warm weather will continue through the 
end of the week as high pressure settles over the region. 

2. Showers and thunderstorms are likely to return to the region 
next week as a series of shortwaves cross the Northeast, ushering in 
seasonably cooler conditions by midweek.

&&

.DISCUSSION...
As of 223 AM EDT Thursday...

KEY MESSAGE 1: An upper trough is pivoting across Quebec and northern 
New England/NY early this morning. Meanwhile, surface high pressure 
is building eastward across the Great Lakes. The upper trough is 
mainly producing just some scattered cloud cover, though some light 
rain has been noted over southern portions of St Lawrence County. 
The incoming airmass associated with the high is quite dry, so 
anticipate this will wane over the next few hours as ridging moves 
overhead. This fair weather will settle over our area for the 
remainder of the week. Each day will feature plentiful sunshine, 
with highs gradually increasing as we head into the weekend. We'll 
start out with highs in the 70s to around 80F today, and topping out 
in the 80s to near 90F by Saturday and Sunday. Thankfully, humidity 
will remain at comfortable levels as dewpoints are expected to be in 
the 40s and 50s, perhaps nudging into the low 60s by Sunday. The dry 
air and mostly clear skies will result in optimal radiational 
cooling tonight and Thursday night, and lows will be in the mid 40s 
to mid 50s both nights. Winds could be a little on the breezy side 
this afternoon, but these should lessen by tonight as the pressure 
gradient slackens. A cold front starts to approach from 
Ontario/Great Lakes later Sunday, and some guidance indicates that a 
few showers/thunderstorms may make it into northern NY before 
sunset. However, general expectations are the bulk of any 
precipitation will hold off until after dark.

KEY MESSAGE 2: Upper level closed low pressure will rotate across the 
Hudson Bay into Quebec for much of next week, sending a series of 
shortwaves through northern New York and Vermont. The first is set 
to move through Sunday night, though high pressure and drier air 
locked in across Nova Scotia looks to hinder its eastward 
progression through the forecast area and limit measurable 
precipitation. Chances of a shower or thunderstorm Sunday night are 
around 0 to 25%, highest across northern New York. Southerly to 
southwesterly flow will keep low temperatures seasonable to warm in 
the mid 50s to mid 60s Sunday night, then allow temps to climb into 
the upper 70s to mid 80s on Monday despite scattered showers and 
thunderstorms (20 to 60% chance precip, highest in northern New 
York) as moisture finally lifts into the region. Lingering showers 
and clouds Monday night will keep temperatures seasonably warm in 
the mid 50s to mid 60s again before a weak surface low crosses the 
Great Lakes and another low pressure strengthens in the mid-Atlantic 
on Tuesday, bringing a more widespread round of showers and 
thunderstorms to the region. Tuesday afternoon looks like the most 
likely period for some showers and thunderstorms with 60-80% chance 
of measurable precipitation as some deterministic models are 
depicting the shortwave as a closed low aloft, but this may change 
as we get closer. As waves pass through next week, they will take 
with them small chunks of heat and moisture, gradually knocking our 
temperatures and humidity levels down into midweek. By next 
Wednesday, highs will be widespread 70s, and lows should fall into 
the 50s and lower 60s, all slightly below seasonal normals.

&&

.AVIATION /12Z THURSDAY THROUGH MONDAY/...
Through 12Z Friday...Dry weather will continue through the end 
of the week as high pressure settles over the region. VFR
conditions are expected to remain in place over the next 24
hours. SLK recently had a period of IFR mist and fog, but has
since seen conditions improve back to IFR as of 11Z this
morning (Thursday). Light and variable to southerly winds this
morning are expected to increase out of the west around 14Z-15Z
Thursday to 5-15 knots with gusts 15-20 knots likely beginning 
14Z-18Z. Thursday night and early Friday morning, there is the
potential for fog formation in the favored valley locations,
with MPV being the most likely spot to see changes in flight
conditions. With the time frame for this being 09Z-12Z Friday
and at the tail end of the 24 hour TAF window, there remains a
fair amount of uncertainty, and the forecast will need to be
refined in later issuances. For now, confidence is not high
enough to include any vis restrictions in the TAFs. 


Outlook...

Friday: VFR.
Friday Night: VFR.
Saturday: VFR.
Saturday Night: VFR.
Sunday: VFR.
Sunday Night: VFR.
Monday: Mainly VFR, with local MVFR possible.

&&

.BTV WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
VT...None.
NY...None.

&&

$$

WHAT HAS CHANGED...Hastings
DISCUSSION...Storm/Hastings
AVIATION...Storm
